intro: JavaScript maximizes developer flexibility through a dynamic runtime and managed garbage collection. Moving to Rust introduces strict compile-time guarantees, zero-cost abstractions, and memory safety without a garbage collector via an explicit ownership model. This migration is vital when building highly optimized engines, WebAssembly targets, or reliability-critical infrastructure services.

diffs.to: Static, strong type rules with affine type ownership validation.
Compile-time ownership analysis checking borrow lifespans.

diffs.from: V8 JIT compilation executing inside an asynchronous loop.
diffs.to: AOT optimized compilation powered by LLVM architectures.
Monadic Result<T, E> and Option<T> type evaluations.

diffs.from: Single thread event execution looping macro tasks.
diffs.to: Data-race-free concurrent threading guaranteed by type bounds.

examples.title: Safe Option Parsing
examples.description: Validating missing data elements without risking runtime errors.

examples.title: Error Bound Transformations

tips.title: Master Ownership Tracking
tips.body: Understand Rust's core concepts of ownership, moves, borrows, and lifespans to work effectively with the borrow checker.

tips.title: Avoid Explicit Nil Allocations
tips.body: Replace null variables with explicit Option enums to intercept missing data during compilation.

tips.title: Propagate Errors with Operators
tips.body: Use the question mark operator (?) to return errors early and simplify nested Result pipelines.

tips.title: Leverage Pattern Matching
tips.body: Deconstruct structures using match or if let statements to ensure all code execution branches are accounted for.
